name: All Else Equal: Making Better Decisions
description: Join Stanford GSB finance professor Jonathan Berk and Jules van Binsbergen of The Wharton School in a conversation with prominent business leaders about common flaws in the decision making process and what to do about them. Learn more at AllElseEqualPodcast.com.All Else Equal: Making Better Decisions Podcast is a production of Stanford Graduate School of Business and is produced by University FM.

recent_episodes.title: Ep73 “The Dangers of Group Think on Decision Making” with Adi Sunderam
recent_episodes.description:
Whether it be in politics, public health, or corporate finance, why are people more likely to interpret facts or data in a way that fits their preconceived notions about the world as opposed to searching for the fundamental truth?  

A new paper from the Harvard Business School called, Sharing Models to Interpret Data (by Joshua Schwartzstein and Adi Sunderam)

studies the propensity for people to adopt interpretations to data based on their community’s beliefs, and why this can lea

recent_episodes.title: Ep72 Alternatives vs. Mutual Funds: Where Should You Put Your Money
recent_episodes.description:
In the rapid-moving world of delegated money management, it is important to recognize the differences in how mutual funds and alternative assets operate. When it comes to alternatives, how do these funds wind up with strong incentive contracts for the money managers as opposed to flat fee contracts more commonly seen in mutual funds? Why do managers of alternatives cap their fund sizes when it could potentially lead to lower fees?

recent_episodes.title: Ep71 “The Working From Home Revolution” with Nick Bloom
recent_episodes.description:
When the pandemic hit in 2020, working from home skyrocketed. Six years later, many companies have returned to in-person work, but hybrid models are still the dominant structure. So with WFH here to stay in some form, what’s the proper balance? And what are we at risk of losing in the long term with less in-office interaction?

Nick Bloom is an economics professor at Stanford University and one of the first researchers to take a serious look at the work from home phenomenon. He joins hos

score_reason: Finance professors interviewing business leaders about decision-making flaws could work for Andreas's data-driven insights and building/exiting an agency, though finance-heavy focus may not be ideal fit.

key_themes: How people's stated beliefs diverge from their actual behavior and decisions
Understanding systemic flaws in decision-making across industries and institutions
Data, evidence, and intelligence as competitive advantages in an increasingly complex economy
How traditional institutions and industries adapt to technological and economic change
enrichment.relevant_episode: Ep73 'The Dangers of Group Think on Decision Making' with Adi Sunderam — directly parallels Andreas's 'say-do gap' thesis and explores why organizations adopt beliefs over evidence, a core problem in traditional industries that fear AI.
enrichment.coverage_gap: How practitioners in non-tech industries can use AI and data to make better decisions without falling into the same groupthink and preference falsification traps the podcast regularly examines.
enrichment.suggested_hook: The companies that turn raw data into actual decisions will outcompete everyone else—and that's happening fastest in the industries that were slowest to trust data in the first place.
